title: Step 1: What Is an Atom? The Smallest Piece of Everything

frames.text:
Everything you see, touch, and breathe is made of atoms.

Your phone. The air. Your body. Even the screen you're looking at right now.

Atoms are so tiny that a single strand of human hair is about **500,000 atoms wide**.
frames.speech: Everything you see, touch, and breathe is made of atoms. Your phone, the air, your body, even this screen right now. Atoms are so tiny that a single strand of human hair is about five hundred thousand atoms wide.

frames.text:
An atom has two regions:

⚛️ **Nucleus** — center, contains protons (+) and neutrons (0)

🌀 **Electron cloud** — electrons (−) orbit the nucleus in shells

Atoms are mostly empty space — if the nucleus were a marble, the electrons would be 1 km away!
frames.speech: An atom has two regions. The nucleus in the center contains protons, which are positively charged, and neutrons, which have no charge. Electrons orbit the nucleus in shells. Atoms are mostly empty space — if the nucleus were the size of a marble, the electrons would orbit about one kilometer away.

title: Step 2: The Bohr Model — Electrons in Shells

frames.text:
Niels Bohr (1913) proposed that electrons orbit in fixed energy levels — called **shells** or **orbits**.

Shell 1 (K) → max 2 electrons
Shell 2 (L) → max 8 electrons
Shell 3 (M) → max 18 electrons

This is the **2-8-18 rule** — the foundation of the periodic table!
frames.speech: Niels Bohr in 1913 proposed that electrons orbit in fixed energy levels called shells. Shell 1 can hold a maximum of 2 electrons. Shell 2 can hold 8. Shell 3 can hold 18. This is the 2-8-18 rule, which is the very foundation of the periodic table.

frames.text:
🧠 Memory trick for shells:

**"Killing Lions Makes Noticeably Odd People"**
→ K(2) → L(8) → M(18) → N(32)

Or just: 2 × 1² = 2,  2 × 2² = 8,  2 × 3² = 18

The formula for max electrons in shell n = **2n²**
frames.speech: Here's a memory trick: Killing Lions Makes Noticeably Odd People — K shell 2, L shell 8, M shell 18, N shell 32. Or just remember the formula: the maximum electrons in shell n equals 2 times n squared.

title: Step 3: Atomic Number, Mass, and Isotopes

followup_questions.question: What is the difference between atoms and molecules?
followup_questions.speech: What exactly is the difference between atoms and molecules?

followup_questions.question: What makes two atoms the same element?
followup_questions.speech: What makes two atoms belong to the same chemical element?

followup_questions.question: What is radioactivity?
followup_questions.speech: What causes some atoms to be radioactive and emit radiation?

frames.text:
Every element is defined by its **atomic number** = number of protons.

Carbon always has 6 protons. Oxygen always has 8.

**Atomic mass** = protons + neutrons

**Isotopes** = same protons, different neutrons (e.g. Carbon-12 vs Carbon-14)

Carbon-14 is used in archaeology to date ancient objects — it decays at a known rate! ☢️
frames.speech: Every element is defined by its atomic number — the number of protons. Carbon always has 6 protons. Oxygen always has 8. Atomic mass equals protons plus neutrons. Isotopes are atoms of the same element but with different numbers of neutrons. For example Carbon-12 versus Carbon-14. Carbon-14 is actually used in archaeology to date ancient objects, because it decays at a known and predictable rate.
